stories.title: Why Cars Cost So Much in Singapore
stories.headline_kid: Today is a special Wednesday when Singapore finds out how much it costs to buy the right to own a car!
summary: Every first and third Wednesday of the month, Singapore holds a car-bidding exercise. People who want a new car must first win something called a Certificate of Entitlement, or COE for short. Think of it like a golden ticket — without it, you cannot legally drive a car in Singapore, no matter how much the car itself costs.

Singapore has very little land, so there is not enough space for unlimited cars on the road. The government limits the number of COEs given out each year to control traffic jams and pollution. This means people must bid against each other, and whoever offers the highest price gets the ticket.

In recent years, COE prices have sometimes climbed above $100,000 — that is more than the price of a small flat! This is one big reason why owning a car in Singapore is so expensive compared to many other countries.
For families thinking about buying a car, today's bidding results matter a lot. It affects whether Mum and Dad's dream car suddenly becomes affordable or stays out of reach for another two weeks.

lenses.me: If your family is hoping to get a car, you might hear your parents talking excitedly (or worriedly) about today's COE results at the dinner table.
lenses.family: Families planning to buy a car must budget not just for the vehicle, but also this extra COE cost, which can sometimes cost more than the car itself.
lenses.friends: Some classmates may notice their families switching to public transport or carpooling instead of buying a car because COE prices are so high.
lenses.nation: The COE system helps Singapore control traffic jams on our small island, keeping roads less crowded than in many big cities.
lenses.world: Singapore's COE system is unique — very few other countries in the world make people bid for the right to own a car, so visitors are often amazed when they learn about it.

compo_words.meaning: the right to have or do something
compo_words.example: Winning the bid gave the family an entitlement to buy a new car.

compo_words.meaning: to offer an amount of money to try to win something
compo_words.example: Many people bid for a Certificate of Entitlement every month.

compo_words.meaning: an extra amount of money paid for something valuable
compo_words.example: The car premium shot up because so many people wanted a COE.

compo_words.meaning: not enough for everyone who wants it
compo_words.example: Because road space is scarce, Singapore limits the number of cars allowed.

compo_words.meaning: to have enough money to pay for something
compo_words.example: Not every family can afford a car once the COE price is added.

compo_words.meaning: too many vehicles crowded together, causing traffic jams
compo_words.example: Fewer cars on the road means less congestion during rush hour.

compo_phrases.phrase: burn a hole in one's pocket
compo_phrases.meaning: to cost so much that it quickly uses up all your money
compo_phrases.example: A high COE price can really burn a hole in a family's pocket.
Many families think twice before buying a car because of the high cost.
A cheap COE bid can feel worth its weight in gold to a hopeful car buyer.

questions: Does your family own a car? How do you think high car prices affect the way your family travels around Singapore?
Do you think it is fair that people have to bid for the right to own a car? Why or why not?
If your family could choose between a car and taking public transport every day, which would you pick? Why?
phrases: One thing I find interesting is ...
If I were in that situation, I would ...
This reminds me of a time when ...

stories.title: Sunny Mornings, Sudden Showers: Why July Feels So Sticky
stories.headline_kid: Ever notice how it's blazing hot in the morning but pouring by afternoon? Here's why Singapore feels extra warm and wet this month!
summary: From around June to September, Singapore experiences what is called the Southwest Monsoon season. During this time, winds blow from the southwest and bring hot, dry mornings followed by sudden, heavy thunderstorms in the afternoon.

The National Environment Agency, or NEA, often reminds everyone to drink plenty of water and avoid too much outdoor activity when it is extremely hot. This is because our bodies can overheat quickly when the air feels humid and sticky, almost like being inside a steamy bathroom.

During this season, smoke haze can sometimes drift over from forest fires in nearby countries like Indonesia, making the sky look grey and hazy instead of blue. When haze levels rise, people with breathing problems are told to stay indoors and schools may cut down on outdoor activities.
Staying safe in this weather is simple: carry a water bottle, wear a hat under the sun, and always have a small umbrella ready for sudden showers on the way home from school.

lenses.me: You might need to carry both a water bottle and a foldable umbrella in your school bag this month, just in case!
lenses.family: Families may need to plan outdoor outings for cooler parts of the day, like early morning or evening, to avoid the harshest heat.
lenses.friends: Your PE lessons or outdoor CCAs might be adjusted or moved indoors if the heat or haze gets too intense.
lenses.nation: Singapore's hospitals and clinics often see more patients with heat exhaustion or breathing troubles during hot, hazy spells, so public health messages become very important.
lenses.world: The haze reminds us that actions in one country, like forest burning, can affect the air that people breathe in neighbouring countries far away.

compo_words.meaning: a season with strong winds that bring particular weather patterns
compo_words.example: The Southwest Monsoon brings hot mornings and stormy afternoons to Singapore.

compo_words.meaning: having a lot of moisture in the air, making it feel sticky
compo_words.example: The humid air made everyone sweat even while sitting still.

compo_words.meaning: an official warning or piece of advice
compo_words.example: NEA issued a heat advisory reminding residents to drink more water.

compo_words.meaning: to move slowly, carried by wind or water
compo_words.example: Smoke haze can drift across the sea from nearby countries.

compo_words.meaning: a state of being extremely tired, often from heat or effort
compo_words.example: Playing football at noon without water can lead to heat exhaustion.

compo_words.meaning: to protect something from harm
compo_words.example: A wide hat can shield your face from the strong afternoon sun.

compo_phrases.meaning: no matter what the weather is like
compo_phrases.example: The parade rehearsal continued, come rain or shine.
Grandma felt under the weather during the hazy days.

compo_phrases.meaning: something refreshing, especially clean air after haze
compo_phrases.example: When the haze cleared, the cool breeze felt like a breath of fresh air.

questions: How do you usually cope with hot and humid weather during your outdoor activities?
Do you think schools should cancel outdoor activities when it is very hazy? Why or why not?
Share about a time when sudden weather, like a heavy thunderstorm, changed your plans for the day.
phrases: It is important to remember that ...

stories.title: Getting Ready for Singapore's 61st Birthday Bash
stories.headline_kid: Hear loud booms and see planes zooming overhead this month? Singapore is rehearsing for its big National Day Parade!
summary: Every year on 9 August, Singapore celebrates its birthday with the National Day Parade, or NDP. In 2026, our country turns 61 years old since becoming independent in 1965. To make sure everything runs smoothly, performers, soldiers and pilots have been rehearsing for weeks before the actual day.

During July, residents across Singapore may hear loud bangs from fireworks tests or spot fighter jets and helicopters practising their flypast in the sky. Some rehearsals even happen at night so the actual parade night feels magical with lights, music and cheering crowds.

Thousands of volunteers and performers, including students, spend months practising dance routines and formations for the parade segment. It takes real teamwork and patience, since everyone must move together perfectly to create beautiful patterns when viewed from above.
The parade is more than just fun and fireworks — it is a time for everyone in Singapore to remember how far our small country has come and to feel proud of being part of one big Singaporean family.

lenses.me: You might get to watch a preview show or fireworks rehearsal with your family, even before the actual National Day!
lenses.family: Some families plan a yearly tradition of watching the parade together on television or heading down to catch a live preview.
lenses.friends: Classmates who are part of school bands, dance groups or the guard-of-honour contingent might be busy rehearsing for NDP after school.
lenses.nation: NDP brings Singaporeans from all races and backgrounds together to celebrate shared values and achievements as one nation.
lenses.world: The parade also welcomes international guests and shows the world how Singapore, though small, can put together a grand, organised celebration.

compo_words.meaning: freedom from being controlled by another country
compo_words.example: Singapore gained independence in 1965.

compo_words.meaning: when aircraft fly together over an area as part of a display
compo_words.example: The crowd cheered as the flypast roared over the Padang.

compo_words.meaning: a group of people representing a larger group, like a school or country
compo_words.example: Her school sent a contingent of drummers to perform at NDP.

compo_words.meaning: to practise something before performing it for real
compo_words.example: The dancers rehearse every weekend to prepare for the big night.

compo_words.meaning: the state of being joined together as one
compo_words.example: NDP celebrates the unity of Singaporeans from different backgrounds.

compo_words.meaning: extremely impressive or dramatic
compo_words.example: The fireworks display was truly spectacular this year.

compo_phrases.meaning: the more you practise, the better you become
compo_phrases.example: The performers reminded themselves that practice makes perfect before the final rehearsal.
The crowd was in high spirits as the parade began.
The night sky lit up with fireworks, truly a sight to behold.

questions: What does National Day mean to you, and how does your family usually celebrate it?
If you could take part in the National Day Parade, which role would you like to play — performer, dancer or spectator? Why?
Why do you think it is important for a country to have a national celebration like NDP?
phrases: To me, National Day is about ...
What I love most about NDP is ...
It makes me feel proud because ...

stories.title: The Buzz About Keeping Dengue Away
stories.headline_kid: One tiny mosquito can make a whole family sick — here's why Singapore is on high alert this season!
summary: Warm, wet weather in Singapore creates the perfect condition for a type of mosquito called Aedes aegypti to breed. This mosquito can spread a serious illness called dengue fever, which causes high fever, body aches and, in serious cases, can be life-threatening.

The National Environment Agency, or NEA, regularly reminds everyone to check their homes for stagnant water — even a small amount in a flowerpot tray or pail can become a mosquito breeding ground within just a few days. This simple habit is called the '5-step mozzie wipeout'.
Schools also play their part by checking classrooms, corridors and gardens for stagnant water, and some may remind students to apply insect repellent, especially during outdoor lessons or CCAs.

Everyone, even children, can help stop dengue by telling an adult if they spot standing water at home, in the corridor, or at the playground. Small actions like this can protect the whole neighbourhood from getting sick.

lenses.me: You can be a dengue-fighting hero by checking your own room and telling your parents if you spot any stagnant water, like in a forgotten cup or vase.
lenses.family: Families are encouraged to do a home check every week, especially clearing water from flowerpot plates, pails and unused containers.
lenses.friends: If a classmate falls sick with dengue, they might miss school for a while to rest and recover, so friends can help by sharing notes and well wishes.
lenses.nation: Singapore works hard as a nation to keep dengue cases low, since a single untreated breeding spot can affect an entire estate.
lenses.world: Dengue is a growing concern in many warm countries around the world, and scientists globally are researching new ways, like special mosquitoes, to fight it.

compo_words.meaning: to reproduce and increase in number
compo_words.example: Mosquitoes breed quickly in still water.

compo_words.meaning: not flowing or moving, often referring to water
compo_words.example: Stagnant water in a pail can attract mosquitoes within days.

compo_words.meaning: a sudden rise in cases of a disease in an area
compo_words.example: Health officials worked quickly to control the dengue outbreak.

compo_words.meaning: a substance used to keep insects away
compo_words.example: She applied insect repellent before her outdoor CCA.

compo_words.meaning: keeping careful watch for possible danger
compo_words.example: Residents were told to stay vigilant and check for stagnant water.

compo_words.meaning: to stop something from happening
compo_words.example: Simple habits at home can help prevent the spread of dengue.

compo_phrases.meaning: to stop a problem early before it grows worse
compo_phrases.example: Clearing stagnant water helps nip a dengue outbreak in the bud.

compo_phrases.meaning: it is wiser to stop a problem before it happens than to fix it later
compo_phrases.example: The NEA campaign reminds everyone that prevention is better than cure.

compo_phrases.meaning: to contribute one's own share of effort
compo_phrases.example: Every family should do their part to keep mosquitoes away.

questions: Have you or someone you know ever experienced dengue fever? What was it like?
What are some things your family does at home to prevent mosquitoes from breeding?
Why do you think it is everyone's responsibility, not just the government's, to fight dengue?
phrases: One way to solve this is ...

stories.title: Team Singapore Gears Up for the Commonwealth Games
stories.headline_kid: In just over a week, Team Singapore's athletes will fly off to compete against the world's best at the Commonwealth Games!
summary: The Commonwealth Games is a huge multi-sport event held every four years, joining athletes from countries that were once part of the British Empire — a group now called the Commonwealth, which includes Singapore, the United Kingdom, Australia, India and many more.

This year's Games are being hosted in Glasgow, Scotland, and are expected to kick off later this month. Singapore usually sends a strong team in sports where our athletes have shone before, such as swimming, table tennis and badminton.

Before big competitions like this, athletes train incredibly hard, often waking up before sunrise for practice and sacrificing time with friends and family to focus on their sport. Coaches, physiotherapists and even sports scientists work behind the scenes to help each athlete perform at their very best.

Whether or not our athletes win medals, simply representing Singapore on such a big stage is already something to be proud of. It shows the whole world that even a small country can produce world-class talent through hard work and determination.

lenses.me: You could try cheering on Team Singapore by watching the Games with your family, just like watching a big football match together.
lenses.family: Families might gather around the television or livestream to support Singaporean athletes competing on the world stage.
lenses.friends: Classmates who play sports like swimming or badminton in their CCA might feel extra inspired watching Team Singapore compete.
lenses.nation: Every medal or personal best achieved brings pride to the nation and encourages more young Singaporeans to take up sports seriously.
lenses.world: The Commonwealth Games bring together dozens of countries in friendly competition, showing how sport can unite people from very different backgrounds.

compo_words.meaning: a group representing a country or organisation at an event
compo_words.example: Singapore's contingent trained for months before the Games.

compo_words.meaning: extremely tiring and demanding
compo_words.example: The swimmer endured a gruelling training schedule before the competition.

compo_words.meaning: to act or speak on behalf of a group, like a country
compo_words.example: It is an honour to represent Singapore at an international competition.

compo_words.meaning: firmness of purpose; not giving up easily
compo_words.example: Her determination helped her recover quickly from an injury before the Games.

compo_words.meaning: to try to win against others in a contest
compo_words.example: Athletes from over seventy countries will compete in Glasgow.

compo_words.meaning: to fill someone with the desire or confidence to do something
compo_words.example: Watching Team Singapore compete can inspire young athletes to train harder.

compo_phrases.meaning: to try as hard as possible
compo_phrases.example: The athletes promised to give it their all for Singapore.

compo_phrases.meaning: despite difficulties that seem impossible to overcome
compo_phrases.example: The young swimmer qualified for the Games against all odds.

compo_phrases.meaning: in front of an international audience
compo_phrases.example: Representing Singapore on the world stage is a dream come true for many athletes.

questions: Do you enjoy watching international sporting events like the Commonwealth Games? Why or why not?
If you were an athlete representing Singapore, which sport would you choose to compete in?
Why do you think it is important for young people to support and encourage local athletes?
phrases: If I had the chance, I would ...
